en.extract:
The western gerygone (Gerygone fusca) is a small, brownish-grey species of passerine bird, which is found in inland and south-west Australia.  It is an arboreal, insectivore of open forest, woodland and dry shrubland.  It is not currently threatened with extinction (IUCN: Least Concern).


Systematics and taxonomy
The western gerygone is a member of the family Acanthizidae (Thornbills and Allies), which has been split from the family Pardalotidae (Pardalotes).
It is a sister-species to the mangrove gerygone (Gerygone levigaster).  The close relationship of this phylogenetic pair is suggested by analyses of both morphological characteristics and genetic loci.  Populations of a common ancestor of the two species are thought to have diverged after becoming fragmented by severe aridity during the Pleistocene.  These two species are now in secondary contact in the Carpentarian Basin, but occupy very different habitats and do not interbreed.
The common name western gerygone and scientific name Gerygone fusca are recognized by the taxonomies of the International Ornithological Congress, Clement's Checklist, the Handbook of the Birds of the World and Christidis and Boles.


Description

The western gerygone has plain, brownish-grey upperparts, with no prominent wing markings.  The underparts are whitish, with variable amounts of grey on the throat and breast.   The outer tail-feathers are conspicuously marked, with large, white patches at the base, a broad, blackish, subterminal tail band and white tips.
It is usually found singly or in pairs, in the mid to upper storey of trees and shrubs and is often located by its characteristic, persistent song.  It can be very active when foraging. 
The western gerygone is similar in appearance to several other Australian gerygones, which don't usually share its habitat.  Its plumage can be distinguished from these species by the diagnostic large, white patches at base of its outer tail feathers.


Distribution
The western gerygone is the most widespread gerygone species and is endemic to Australia.  Its three subspecies show subtle differences in plumage and form geographically separate populations:

Subspecies fusca is found in south-west Western Australia.
Subspecies exsul is found in eastern Australia; from the Carpentarian Basin, through central and western Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria, and eastern South Australia.  An isolated, resident population from the Eyre Peninsula in South Australia is usually ascribed to this subspecies.
Subspecies mungi is found in central Australia; in the interior of Western Australia, the Northern Territory and South Australia.
Nomadic individuals may travel far beyond this species' regular geographic limits.
Gerygone species are largely allopatric.  They are usually separated from each other by their geographic distribution, or by their preference for different habitats.  As it is the only gerygone of the Australian interior, the western gerygone does not overlap geographically with other gerygones throughout most of its range. 
There are two island populations.  Both are near Perth in Western Australia. Rottnest Island was colonized by the western gerygone in the 1950s.  It was first observed on the island in 1955 and rapidly spread into all suitable habitat. On nearby Garden Island, which is closer to the Australian mainland, the species has been present since European records began.


Ecology and behaviour


Habitat
The western gerygone occupies a wide range of wooded habitats.  These vary from open sclerophyll forests, dominated by a broad array of eucalyptus species, to sparse mallee and mulga shrublands.  It is often found along watercourses.  In elevated regions, it only occurs below 850 meters.


Movement
Different populations of the western gerygone show different patterns of movement.  Those in south-western Western Australia are partial migrants.  They breed only in the south-west, but some individuals migrate inland or northwards during winter.  Populations in the Carpentarian Basin and on the Eyre Peninsula are sedentary.  Desert populations are partially nomadic, responding to inland rainfall.


Foraging
The western gerygone is insectivorous.  Its foraging techniques include probing into bark, gleaning from foliage, hovering outside foliage and aerial strikes from perches.  It may join other small birds in mixed-species feeding flocks. 


Reproduction

Breeding usually occurs between September and January, but has been recorded from August to March. Courtship involves intricate chases between pairs. Territories are maintained throughout the breeding season and territorial disputes involve agitated calls. Males display by intensely fluttering their wings and tail, with their bodies tilted horizontally.
The nest is a long, oval-shaped, pendent structure, with a hooded entrance near the top and a 'tail' at the bottom. Both sexes build the nest.
The clutch size is 2 or 3 eggs, (rarely 4).  The incubation period lasts 10–12 days, and only the female incubates.  The nestling period lasts 10–13 days, and both parents feed the nestlings.  Fledglings are fed by their parents for up to 15 days, until independence.


Song

The western gerygone's song is an irregular series of clear, high-pitched whistles, with a meandering melody.  Each note maintains a consistent pitch and there is a distinct change in pitch between notes.  Although the song isn't loud in volume, its persistence and distinctive tonal qualities are often recognizable from long distances.  Singing birds may turn their head in different directions with each note.
Across different mainland populations, songs are fairly similar.  Singing is mostly confined to the breeding season and this species is far less conspicuous when it is not breeding.


Song from the colony on Rottnest Island

A distinct, new song has emerged in the western gerygone population which colonized Rottnest Island in the 1950s.  Unlike the mainland song, its notes are delivered in a strictly repeated melody.  (See sound files on right for comparison.)  In 2003, it was estimated that more than a third of the western gerygones on Rottnest island sang the new song, including some individuals which sang both the new song and the typical mainland song.
The island biogeography of birdsong is of interest to evolutionary biologists because of its relevance to speciation.  The novel western gerygone song on Rottnest Island is a notable example of both cultural innovation and cultural transmission by social learning.  It has occurred over a rapid period of time in a recently isolated population. Sexual selection could eventually result in the typical, mainland western gerygone song on Rottnest Island being completely replaced with the novel song.  If secondary contact is subsequently established with the original, mainland population, breeding birds may no longer respond to each other's songs.  Behavioural reproductive isolation is a mechanism of evolutionary divergence.


Status, threats and conservation
The western gerygone is common throughout much of its range, especially in Southwest Australia.  Extensive clearing of native vegetation in this region has led to a reduction in abundance.  Predation of western gerygones by feral cats is thought to be uncommon.
The Australian inland reaches extremely high temperatures in summer. Heat waves in these regions can result in sudden, dramatic, large-scale avian mortality events, with lasting ecological consequences.  The frequency of such events is predicted to increase dramatically in coming decades, due to climate change.  This poses a threat to Australia's inland birds, potentially including some western gerygone populations.
Despite a declining population trend, the western gerygone's conservation status is categorized as least concern by the IUCN and by most Australian state legislation.  This species occupies a wide variety of habitats across a large geographic range, which encompasses numerous protected areas, including large, secure national parks.


Gallery


References

The '''western gerygone''' ('''''Gerygone fusca''''') is a small, brownish-grey [[species]] of [[passerine]] bird, which is found in inland and [[Southwest Australia|south-west]] Australia.<ref name="Menkhorst">{{cite book |last1=Menkhorst |first1=Peter |last2=Rogers |first2=Danny |last3=Clarke |first3=Rohan |last4=Davies |first4=Jeff |last5=Marsack |first5=Peter |last6=Franklin |first6=Kim |title=The Australian Bird Guide |date=2017 |publisher=CSIRO Publishing |location=Melbourne |isbn=978-0-643-09754-4 |edition=1st}}</ref>  It is an arboreal, [[insectivore]] of open [[forest]], [[woodland]] and dry [[shrubland]].<ref name= "HANZAB6">{{cite book |last1= Higgins|first1= P.J. |last2= Peter|first2= J.M.|date= 2002|title= Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds. Volume 6: Pardalotes to Shrike-thrushes. |pages= 360–374|publisher= Oxford University Press|location= Melbourne |isbn= 0-19-553762-9}}</ref>  It is not currently threatened with [[extinction]] ([[IUCN Red List|IUCN]]: [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]]).<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

==Systematics and taxonomy==
The western gerygone is a member of the family [[Acanthizidae]] (Thornbills and Allies), which has been split from the family [[Pardalotidae]] (Pardalotes).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Winkler |first1=D.W. |last2=Billerman |first2=S.M |last3=Lovette |first3=I.J |title=Pardalotes (Pardalotidae), version 1.0.| website=Birds of the World (S. M. Billerman, B. K. Keeney, P. G. Rodewald, and T. S. Schulenberg, Editors) |date=2020|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.pardal2.01 |s2cid=241985695 }}</ref>

It is a [[Sister species|sister-species]] to the [[mangrove gerygone]] (''Gerygone levigaster'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Garcia-Navas |first1=Vicente |last2=Rodriguez-Rey |first2=Marta |last3=Marki |first3=Petter Z. |last4=Christidis |first4=Les |title=Environmental determinism, and not interspecific competition, drives morphological variability in Australasian warblers (Acanthizidae) |journal=Ecology and Evolution |date=2018 |volume=8 |issue=8 |pages=3871–3882|doi=10.1002/ece3.3925 |pmid=29721264 |pmc=5916309 |bibcode=2018EcoEv...8.3871G }}</ref>  The close relationship of this [[Phylogenetic tree|phylogenetic]] pair is suggested by analyses of both [[Morphology (biology)|morphological]] characteristics<ref name="Ford 86">{{cite journal |last1=Ford |first1=Julian |title=Phylogeny of the Acanthizid Warbler Genus ''Gerygone'' Based on Numerical Analyses of Morphological Characters |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=1986 |volume=86 |issue=1 |pages=12–22 |doi=10.1071/MU9860012 |bibcode=1986EmuAO..86...12F }}</ref> and [[genetic loci]].<ref name="Nyari and Joseph">{{cite journal |last1=Nyari |first1=Arpad S. |last2=Joseph |first2=Leo |title=Evolution in Australasian Mangrove Forests: Multilocus Phylogenetic Analysis of the ''Gerygone'' Warblers (Aves: Acanthizidae) |journal=PLOS ONE |date=2012 |volume=7 |issue=2 |article-number=e31840|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0031840 |pmid=22363748 |pmc=3280719 |bibcode=2012PLoSO...731840N |doi-access=free }}</ref>  Populations of a common ancestor of the two species are thought to have [[divergent evolution|diverged]] after becoming fragmented by severe aridity during the [[Pleistocene]].<ref name="Ford 81">{{cite journal|last1=Ford|first1=Julian|date=1981|title=Morphological and Behavioural Evolution in Populations of the Gerygone Fusca Complex|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=2|issue=81|pages=57–81|doi=10.1071/MU9810057 |bibcode=1981EmuAO..81...57F }}</ref>  These two species are now in [[secondary contact]] in the [[Gulf Country|Carpentarian Basin]], but occupy very different habitats and do not [[interbreed]].<ref name="Ford 81" />

The common name western gerygone and scientific name ''Gerygone fusca'' are recognized by the taxonomies of the [[Birds of the World: Recommended English Names|International Ornithological Congress]],<ref name="IOC" /> [[The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World|Clement's Checklist]],<ref name="Clements">{{cite web |last1=Schulenberg |first1=Thomas S. |last2=Iliff |first2=Marshall J. |last3=Billerman |first3=Shawn M. |last4=Sullivan |first4=Brian L. |last5=Wood |first5=Christopher L. |last6=Fredericks |first6=Thomas A. |title=Clement's Checklist |url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/overview-august-2019/ |publisher=The Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA|date=August 2019}}</ref> the [[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]<ref>{{cite book |editor1=Joseph del Hoyo |editor2=Andrew Elliott |editor3=David A Christie |title=The Handbook of the Birds of the World.Volume 12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees |date=2008 |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |isbn=978-84-96553-45-3}}</ref> and Christidis and Boles.<ref name="Christidis and Boles">{{cite book |last1=Christidis |first1=Les |last2=Boles |first2=Walter E. |title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds |date=2008 |publisher=CSIRO Publishing |location=Melbourne |isbn=978-0-643-09602-8 |edition=2nd}}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:Western Gerygone (subspecies fusca). Graham Ringer.jpg|thumb|left|Western gerygone subspecies '''''G. f. fusca'''''. Note the distinctive tail pattern as it fans its tail, while hovering.]]

The western gerygone has plain, brownish-grey upperparts, with no prominent wing markings.<ref name="Menkhorst" />  The underparts are whitish, with variable amounts of grey on the throat and breast.<ref name="Menkhorst" />   The outer [[Flight feather#Rectrices|tail-feathers]] are conspicuously marked, with large, white patches at the base, a broad, blackish, [[List of terms used in bird topography|subterminal tail band]] and white tips.<ref name="Pizzey">{{cite book|last1=Pizzey|first1=Graham|title=The Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|last2=Knight|first2=Frank|date=2003|publisher=Harper Collins|isbn=978-0-7322-9193-8|location=Sydney}}</ref>

It is usually found singly or in pairs,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Chisholm |first1=E. C. |title=Birds of the Pilliga Scrub |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=1936 |volume=36 |issue=1 |pages=32–38 |doi=10.1071/MU936032 |bibcode=1936EmuAO..36...32C }}</ref> in the mid to upper storey of trees and shrubs<ref name="HANZAB6" /> and is often located by its characteristic, persistent song.<ref name="HANZAB6" />  It can be very active when foraging.<ref name="Chisholm">{{cite journal |last1=Chisholm |first1=E. C. |title=The Birds of Barellan, New South Wales With Botanical and Other Notes |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=1938 |volume=37 |issue=4 |pages=301–313 |doi=10.1071/MU937301 |bibcode=1938EmuAO..37..301C }}</ref> 

The western gerygone is similar in appearance to several other Australian gerygones, which don't usually share its habitat.<ref name="Keast" />  Its plumage can be distinguished from these species by the diagnostic large, white patches at base of its outer tail feathers.<ref name="Menkhorst" />

== Distribution ==

The western gerygone is the most widespread [[gerygone]] species and is [[endemic]] to Australia.<ref name="HANZAB6" />  Its three subspecies show subtle differences in plumage and form geographically separate populations:<ref name="HANZAB6" />
:Subspecies '''''fusca''''' is found in south-west [[Western Australia]].<ref name="Clements" />

:Subspecies '''''exsul''''' is found in eastern Australia; from the [[Gulf Country|Carpentarian Basin]], through central and western [[Queensland]], [[New South Wales]] and [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]], and eastern [[South Australia]].<ref name="Clements" />  An isolated, resident population from the [[Eyre Peninsula]] in South Australia is usually ascribed to this subspecies.<ref name="Reid" />

:Subspecies '''''mungi''''' is found in central Australia; in the interior of Western Australia, the [[Northern Territory]] and South Australia.<ref name="Clements" />

Nomadic individuals may travel far beyond this species' regular geographic limits.<ref name="Reid">{{cite journal|last1=Reid|first1=Julian|last2=Brissenden|first2=Piers|last3=Puckridge|first3=Jim|last4=Carpenter|first4=Graham|last5=Paton|first5=Penny|date=1997|title=Comments on the Distribution of Five Bird Species in the Flinders Ranges: Some New Data and a Reappraisal of Historical Records|journal=South Austr. Orn.|volume=32|issue=7|pages=113–118}}</ref>

Gerygone species are largely [[Allopatric speciation|allopatric]].<ref name="Keast">{{cite journal |last1=Keast |first1=Allen |last2=Recher |first2=Harry F. |title=The Adaptive Zone of the Genus Gerygone (Acanthizidae) as Shown by Morphology and Feeding Habits |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=1997 |volume=1 |issue=97 |pages=1–17 |doi=10.1071/MU97001 |bibcode=1997EmuAO..97....1K }}</ref>  They are usually separated from each other by their geographic distribution, or by their preference for different habitats.<ref name="Keast" />  As it is the only gerygone of the Australian interior, the western gerygone does not overlap geographically with other gerygones throughout most of its range.<ref name="Menkhorst" /> 

There are two island populations.<ref name="Ford 81" />  Both are near [[Perth]] in Western Australia.<ref name="Ford 81" /> [[Rottnest Island]] was colonized by the western gerygone in the 1950s.<ref name="Saunders">{{cite journal|last1=Saunders|first1=D.A.|last2=de Rebeira|first2=C.P.|date=1985|title=Turnover in Breeding Bird Populations on Rottnest I., Western Australia|journal= Wildlife Research|volume=12|issue=3 |pages=467–77|doi=10.1071/WR9850467 |bibcode=1985WildR..12..467S }}</ref>  It was first observed on the island in 1955 and rapidly spread into all suitable habitat.<ref name="Baker 2003">{{cite journal |last1=Baker |first1=Myron C. |last2=Baker |first2=Merrill S. |last3=Baker |first3=Esther M. |title=Rapid evolution of a novel song and an increase in repertoire size in an island population of an Australian songbird |journal=Ibis |date=2003 |volume=145 |issue=465–471|pages=465–471 |doi=10.1046/j.1474-919X.2003.00190.x }}</ref> On nearby [[Garden Island, Western Australia|Garden Island]], which is closer to the Australian mainland, the species has been present since European records began.<ref name="Ford 81" />

==Ecology and behaviour==

===Habitat===
The western gerygone occupies a wide range of wooded habitats.<ref name="Ford 81" />  These vary from open [[sclerophyll forest]]s, dominated by a broad array of [[eucalyptus]] species, to sparse [[Mallee Woodlands and Shrublands|mallee]] and [[Mulga (habitat)|mulga]] shrublands.<ref name="Ford 81" />  It is often found along watercourses.<ref name="Ford 81" />  In elevated regions, it only occurs below 850 meters.<ref name="HANZAB6" />

===Movement===
Different populations of the western gerygone show different patterns of movement.<ref name="Ford 81" />  Those in south-western Western Australia are partial [[Bird migration|migrants]].<ref name="Ford 81" />  They breed only in the south-west, but some individuals migrate inland or northwards during winter.<ref name="Ford 81" />  Populations in the Carpentarian Basin and on the Eyre Peninsula are sedentary.<ref name="Ford 81" />  Desert populations are partially nomadic, responding to inland rainfall.<ref name="Ford 81" />

===Foraging===
The western gerygone is [[Insectivore|insectivorous]].<ref name="HANZAB6" />  Its [[foraging]] techniques include probing into bark, gleaning from foliage, hovering outside foliage and [[Hawking (birds)|aerial strikes]] from perches.<ref name="Keast" />  It may join other small birds in [[Mixed species flocks|mixed-species feeding flocks]].<ref name="Chisholm" /> 

===Reproduction===
[[File:Gerygone fusca exsul eggs from Balranald NSW in Museums Victoria 1976.jpg|thumb|right|Western gerygone eggs from [[Balranald]], NSW (Museums Victoria)]]

Breeding usually occurs between September and January, but has been recorded from August to March.<ref name="BOTW">{{cite journal|last1=Gregory|first1=P|date=2020|title=Western Gerygone (Gerygone fusca) version 1.0|website=Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors)|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY|doi=10.2173/bow.wesger1.01|s2cid=216409673}}</ref> [[Courtship display|Courtship]] involves intricate chases between pairs.<ref name="HANZAB6" /> [[Territory (animal)|Territories]] are maintained throughout the breeding season and territorial disputes involve agitated calls.<ref name="HANZAB6" /> Males display by intensely fluttering their wings and tail, with their bodies tilted horizontally.<ref name="BOTW" />

The [[Bird nest|nest]] is a long, oval-shaped, [[Bird nest#Pendent|pendent]] structure, with a hooded entrance near the top and a 'tail' at the bottom.<ref name="BOTW" /> Both sexes build the nest.<ref name="BOTW" />

The [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size is 2 or 3 eggs, (rarely 4).<ref name="HANZAB6" />  The [[Egg incubation|incubation]] period lasts 10–12 days, and only the female incubates.<ref name="BOTW" />  The nestling period lasts 10–13 days, and both parents feed the nestlings.<ref name="BOTW" />  Fledglings are fed by their parents for up to 15 days, until independence.<ref name="HANZAB6" />

===Song===
[[File:Western gerygone mainland song.wav|thumb|Western gerygone: Typical mainland song from [[Walpole, Western Australia|Walpole]], Western Australia.]]
The western gerygone's song is an irregular series of clear, high-pitched whistles, with a meandering melody.<ref name="Menkhorst" />  Each note maintains a consistent pitch and there is a distinct change in pitch between notes.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  Although the song isn't loud in volume, its persistence and distinctive tonal qualities are often recognizable from long distances.<ref name="HANZAB6" />  Singing birds may turn their head in different directions with each note.<ref name="HANZAB6" />

Across different mainland populations, songs are fairly similar.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  Singing is mostly confined to the breeding season and this species is far less conspicuous when it is not breeding.<ref name="HANZAB6" />

====Song from the colony on Rottnest Island====
[[File:Western gerygone Rottnest Island.wav|thumb|Western gerygone: Distinctive song from [[Rottnest Island]].]]
A distinct, new song has emerged in the western gerygone population which colonized [[Rottnest Island]] in the 1950s.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  Unlike the mainland song, its notes are delivered in a strictly repeated melody.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  ''(See sound files on right for comparison.)''  In 2003, it was estimated that more than a third of the western gerygones on Rottnest island sang the new song, including some individuals which sang both the new song and the typical mainland song.<ref name="Baker 2003" />

The [[Insular biogeography|island biogeography]] of [[bird vocalization|birdsong]] is of interest to [[Evolutionary biology|evolutionary biologists]] because of its relevance to [[speciation]].<ref name="Baker 2006">{{cite journal |last1=Baker |first1=Myron C. |title=Differentiation of Mating Vocalizations in Birds: Acoustic Features in Mainland and Island Populations and Evidence of Habitat-Dependent Selection on Songs |journal=Ethology |date=2006 |volume=112 |issue=8 |pages=757–771|doi=10.1111/j.1439-0310.2006.01212.x |bibcode=2006Ethol.112..757B }}</ref>  The novel western gerygone song on Rottnest Island is a notable example of both [[Animal culture|cultural]] innovation and cultural [[Animal culture#Mechanisms of cultural transmission in animals|transmission]] by [[Social learning in animals|social learning]].<ref name="Baker 2003" />  It has occurred over a rapid period of time in a recently isolated population.<ref name="Baker 2003" /> [[Sexual selection]] could eventually result in the typical, mainland western gerygone song on Rottnest Island being completely replaced with the novel song.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  If [[secondary contact]] is subsequently established with the original, mainland population, breeding birds may no longer respond to each other's songs.<ref name="Baker 2003" />  Behavioural [[reproductive isolation]] is a mechanism of [[evolutionary divergence]].<ref name="Mayr">Mayr, E. 1963. ''Animal species and evolution''. Harvard University Press, Cambridge.</ref>

==Status, threats and conservation==
The western gerygone is common throughout much of its range, especially in [[Southwest Australia]].<ref name="Pizzey" />  Extensive clearing of native vegetation in this region has led to a reduction in abundance.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Masters |first1=J. R. |last2=Milhinch |first2=A. L. |title=Birds of the Shire of Northam, About 100 Km East of Perth, Wa |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=1974 |volume=74 |issue=4 |pages=228–244|doi=10.1071/MU974228 |bibcode=1974EmuAO..74..228M }}</ref>  Predation of western gerygones by [[feral cats]] is thought to be uncommon.<ref name="HANZAB6" />

The Australian inland reaches [[List of extreme temperatures in Australia|extremely]] high [[Australian climate#Temperatures|temperatures]] in summer.<ref name="Heat Waves" /> [[Heat waves]] in these regions can result in sudden, dramatic, large-scale avian mortality events, with lasting ecological consequences.<ref name="Heat Waves">{{cite journal |last1=McKechnie |first1=Andrew E. |last2=Hockey |first2=Philip A.R. |last3=Blair |first3=O. |title=Feeling the heat: Australian landbirds and climate change |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |date=2012 |volume=112 |issue=2 |pages=i-vii |doi=10.1071/MUv112n2_ED |bibcode=2012EmuAO.112D...1M |hdl=2263/21912 |s2cid=44810836 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>  The frequency of such events is predicted to increase dramatically in coming decades, due to [[climate change]].  This poses a threat to Australia's inland birds, potentially including some western gerygone populations.<ref name="Heat Waves" />

Despite a declining population trend,<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> the western gerygone's conservation status is categorized as [[Least-concern species|least concern]] by the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN]]<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> and by most Australian state legislation.<ref>{{cite web |title=Species Lists: Conservation List |url=https://lists.ala.org.au/public/speciesLists?max=25&sort=listName&order=asc&listType=eq%3ACONSERVATION_LIST&q=&offset=0 |website=The Atlas of Living Australia |access-date=13 June 2021}}</ref>  This species occupies a wide variety of habitats across a large geographic range,<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> which encompasses numerous [[Protected areas of Australia|protected areas]], including large, secure [[national parks]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Ownership of protected areas |url=http://www.environment.gov.au/land/nrs/about-nrs/ownership#levels |website=Australian Government Department of Agriculture Water and the Environment |access-date=14 June 2021}}</ref>
